stimulus: “Bells of the Returning Tide” is an early 1900s poem by Eira Carwyn. In the poem, the speaker describes experiencing a recurring rhythm of hope followed by sorrowful looking back: ______blank
stem: Which quotation from “Bells of the Returning Tide” most effectively illustrates the claim?

answerOptions.content: “What does this tolling sound proclaim, / That falling on the harbor stones / Brings to my breast a touch of shame / And anxious tones?”
“The rain has paused its sliding flight, / The wind dropped to a hollow sigh, / A solemn hush attends the night, / The gulls go by.”
“It speaks of many a wasted noon, / Of letters torn and gardens dry, / Of harvests gathered all too soon, / Of years gone by.”
“And so the tides keep turning still, / Each, rising, lifts a bright belief, / And each, retreating, leaves a chill / Bound to my grief.”

rationale: Choice D is the best answer because this quotation depicts the speaker experiencing a recurring rhythm: each rising tide lifts “a bright belief” (hope) and each retreating tide leaves “a chill / Bound to my grief” (sorrowful looking back). The phrasing “keep turning still,” “each, rising,” and “each, retreating” signals an ongoing cycle. Choice A is incorrect because it describes a single moment of unease at the sound of bells, not a recurring rhythm. Choice B is incorrect because, although it sets a hushed mood, it shows no cycle of hope and sorrow. Choice C is incorrect because it expresses regret only, with no element of hope or recurring rhythm.
